Q1: Honestly, my study routine was all over the place. I'd tell myself I was going to sit down and focus for a few hours, and sometimes I'd actually log in and watch videos or look at materials, but my mind would wander constantly. I put in about 31 hours a week supposedly studying, but a lot of that time I was just... present, not really absorbing anything. I'd have all these tabs open, the LMS running, but I felt disconnected from what I was doing most of the time.
Q2: My pattern was incredibly inconsistent. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything until a deadline loomed, and then I'd scramble. Other times I'd log in frequently but without any real purpose—just clicking around feeling like I should be doing something. The regularity was more about guilt than actual planning. When I had other things going on in my life, studying was the first thing to slip.
Q3: Usually it was anxiety that made me log in. I'd feel this wave of panic about falling behind, so I'd open the LMS to reassure myself I was doing something. Sometimes it was deadline reminders or seeing a notification. Occasionally I genuinely wanted to learn something, but more often it was this compulsive need to feel like I wasn't completely failing, even though the exam results showed otherwise.
Q4: For a while, logging in did become routine—I'd do it first thing in the morning with my coffee, scroll through updates, watch a video or two. But it was more like checking social media than actual studying. The habit formed because it was easy and made me feel productive without requiring deep focus. Eventually though, even that routine felt hollow because I wasn't retaining anything.
Q5: The videos were the most useful for me because I'm a visual learner. I watched over four hours of content, and when I was actually paying attention, the visual explanations helped concepts click. Discussion posts were okay too—sometimes reading what other students wrote helped me understand things from different angles. But honestly, even the useful resources didn't help enough because my engagement was so surface-level.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. They felt overwhelming, and I'd download them with good intentions but never actually read them. The discussion forums I participated in minimally—only 11 posts—because interacting with others made my anxiety spike. I always felt like I'd say something stupid or reveal how lost I actually was.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was visual and relatively short. Anything that promised quick understanding without requiring me to read dense text or think too hard. I know that sounds terrible, but when you're already struggling mentally, you gravitate toward whatever feels manageable. If something seemed like it would take hours of focused effort, I'd put it off indefinitely.
Q8: Optional activities felt like nice-to-haves that I could skip without consequences, so I usually did skip them. Required ones carried this weight of obligation that sometimes motivated me but more often just added to my stress. The irony is I probably needed the optional practice more than anyone, but I couldn't bring myself to do extra when I was barely managing the basics.
Q9: Yes, I downloaded a lot of materials to study offline. I have good resources at home, so I'd save PDFs and video files thinking I'd review them later without distractions. I also used some external YouTube videos and websites when the course materials didn't make sense. My notes were scattered across different notebooks and apps, none of which the LMS could track.
Q10: There were many times I'd be sitting with my downloaded notes, trying to make sense of concepts, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd rewatch videos I'd already downloaded, or I'd be staring at my handwritten summaries trying to memorize things. Sometimes I'd spend hours in this kind of offline study that looked like complete inactivity online. Not that it helped my exam score much.
Q11: Definitely. I'd access a lecture video once, download it, and then rewatch it multiple times offline when I felt up to it. Same with slides and readings—I'd grab them in one session and then work with them over several days without logging back in. The LMS only captured that initial download, not the hours I spent with those materials afterward.
Q12: There were definitely periods where engaging became nearly impossible. My anxiety would spike, I'd feel overwhelmed by everything, and I'd just... shut down. Sometimes it was personal stuff outside of school, sometimes it was the accumulation of feeling behind and hopeless. The CBT sessions helped a bit, but there were still dark weeks where I could barely function.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted or stressed, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen. I have other responsibilities and extracurriculars that competed for my energy, and honestly, when I was depleted, the course suffered first. Confusion about the material made it worse—I'd open something, feel lost immediately, and close it out of frustration. The fatigue was constant.
Q14: All the time. I'd have videos playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them. My task completion rate looks decent at 88%, but so much of that was going through motions without genuine engagement. I was physically present in the LMS but mentally checked out more often than not.
Q15: I postponed the harder assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The anxiety made it feel impossible to start—like if I tried and failed, it would confirm I wasn't smart enough. So I'd avoid them until the last possible moment, then rush through them. It's self-sabotage, I know. The CBT helped me recognize this pattern, but recognizing it didn't always stop it.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. Without them, I would have drifted indefinitely. But they also caused panic spirals where I'd cram everything at once, which meant lower quality work and worse retention. My assignment scores were decent because deadline pressure forced action, but my exam score shows I never really learned the material deeply.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback would give me a brief boost, but negative feedback—or even constructive criticism—would send me into a spiral. I'd avoid checking grades for days because I was scared of what I'd see. When I did get feedback, it sometimes motivated me to try harder, but other times it just confirmed my fears that I wasn't good enough.
Q18: The course structure with 20 online modules felt overwhelming from the start. There was so much content that I never felt like I could catch up. The flexibility was supposed to help, but for someone like me, it just meant more opportunities to fall behind. I needed more structure, more check-ins, more accountability than the course provided.
Q19: The visual elements and videos made engagement easier—that matched how I learn. But the sheer volume of content made it harder. There wasn't enough scaffolding or guidance on what to prioritize. Everything felt equally important and equally overwhelming. Smaller, more structured modules with clearer pathways would have helped me stay on track instead of drowning.
Q20: That's basically me. High activity because I was logging in constantly, watching videos, clicking around—but the actual submitted work and exam performance didn't match. I think it happens when someone is trying but struggling, or when they're using the LMS as a comfort behavior rather than actual studying. Activity doesn't equal learning, especially when your mind isn't really there.
Q21: I can see how that would happen for some people—they study efficiently offline and only use the LMS for essentials. That wasn't my experience though. My low performance came despite high LMS activity because my engagement was shallow. But someone who's focused and studies effectively from downloaded materials could definitely perform well without much visible online activity.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you log in, what you click, how long you spend. But they can't show whether you're actually thinking or just staring blankly at the screen. They miss all the offline studying, the mental state behind the clicks, the quality of engagement versus just the quantity. They see the surface but not what's underneath.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with what you're learning—not just going through motions. It means the material sticks, you think about it outside of study time, you want to understand it. For me, real engagement was rare this semester. I was present but not engaged. There's a huge difference between showing up and actually being there mentally and emotionally.
Q24: The LMS can't show how sad and anxious I was throughout this course. It can't show the hours I spent staring at materials without comprehending them, or the times I cried from frustration. It doesn't capture the effort it took just to log in some days, or how my mental health made everything ten times harder. The data shows what I did, not what it cost me to do it.
